Various modulation formats including 64-QAM (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation), QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying), 8-PSK, and 16-QAM are considered over satellite channel models. To accurately model real-world impairments, a flexible Lutz model is considered, with parameters optimized to match measurements across frequency bands like L and UHF. Extensive Monte Carlo simulations quantify bit error rate (BER) performance under different code/modulation configurations and channel conditions.

Title: An Upper Bound of Block Error Rate for Finite-Length Coding in Massive MISO Systems
Authors: Xinwei Xu, Dengsheng Lin, Yue Xiao
Abstract: In this contribution, we provide an upper bound of block error rate (BLER) for massive multiple-input single-output (MISO) antenna systems when the number of transmission antennas approaches infinity. In our model, we investigate a coding method suitable for a finite-length code in MISO systems. Specifically, an antenna transmission power allocation algorithm is derived for the MISO system first, which ensures that the encoded symbols on every transmission antenna are over the equivalent channels with the same signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Furthermore, we introduce the orthogonal Hadamard coding and develop the upper bound of the BLER for the finite-length code. Finally, the proposed upper bound for finite-length code is validated through simulations, showing its approximation to both the dispersion bound and the Shannon bound when the code length is short with low code rate.

Title: Blind Estimation of Polar Encoder Parameters
Authors: Aman Gupta, Swaminathan R.
Abstract: Forward error correction (FEC) codes are widely used to correct the errors in the transmitted data and it improves the reliability of the digital transmission. In a practical communication system, accurate information about the channel coding technique is required at the receiver side. However, in certain applications and future generation communication systems, it is mandatory to propose automatic code recognition techniques at the receiver in order to successfully decode the received information signals. In this paper, we propose two algorithms for blind estimation of codeword length and code dimension of polar codes, which are included as codes for the control channels in the fifth generation (5G) wireless standard. The two algorithms work in a sequence, where the first algorithm will estimate the value of codeword length and the second algorithm will estimate the value of code dimension. The proposed algorithms are compared with the existing algorithms in the literature in terms of accuracy of estimation of code parameters. The results of the comparative analysis and the test case results based on the proposed algorithms are reported.

Title: Improved Reliability of Enhanced Sparse Vector Coding Using Maximal Ratio Transmission for Short Packet Transmission
Authors: Dolly Kumari, Mahima Chaudhary, Risham Doley, Shravan Kumar Bandari
Abstract: The increasing prevalence of unmanned services in recent times demands efficient wireless communication schemes with stringent requirements of ultra-reliability and ultra-low latency. Recently, sparse vector coding (SVC), a short packet transmission method, was suggested for highly reliable and fast B5G communications. The key idea in SVC is to transform the packet information into a sparse vector of fewer non-zero positions. To further enhance the reliability, an enhanced SVC (ESVC) technique is proposed in which information is encoded in both non-zero locations of a sparse vector as well as symbols. In this paper, we explore the maximal ratio transmission (MRT) at the base station assuming ESVC as a short packet transmission. Computer-simulated results suggest that the block error rate (BLER) of a short packet decreases by increasing the number of transmitting antennas at the BS under realistic channel models. Results suggest that the proposed technique outperforms the conventional SVC-MRT by 2dB at a BLER of 10−4.

Title: Critical-Set-Aided Multi-Bit Successive Cancellation List Decoders for Polar Codes
Authors: Shan Chen, Shan Cao, Limin Jiang, Zhiyuan Jiang
Abstract: The utilization of 5G and beyond wireless communication systems in autonomous driving and remote healthcare has necessitated the development of channel codes with high reliability and low latency. The multi-bit successive cancellation list (MSCL) parallel decoding of polar codes has shown great potential in the decrease of decoding latency while maintaining a high level of reliability, in comparison to the conventional successive cancellation list (SCL) decoding. However, the complexity of decoding path sorting increases exponentially with the increase of parallelism, which poses significant limitations to the improvement of parallelism. To address this issue, this paper proposes a critical set aided multi-bit successive cancellation list (CS-MSCL) decoding algorithm. Critical sets are introduced to the MSCL decoding, which prunes less reliable candidate paths to guarantee high decoding performance. Meanwhile, dynamic configuration is allowed according to the application scenario to make a good balance in decoding latency, error correction performance, and algorithmic complexity. Simulation results demonstrate that the algorithmic complexity decreased by 1099.04% compared to MSCL decoding for (1024, 512) polar codes when the degree of parallelism is 8 and the list size is 16.

Title: Doppler-Frequency Domain Transmission Based on OFDM
Authors: Ziheng Wei, Zhisong Bie, Jinhong Jiang
Abstract: In this paper, a new D-F domain transmission based on OFDM system is proposed. The signal is transmitted in the Doppler frequency domain, and the signal is transformed in a two-dimensional way, which retains the frequency diversity gain and can resist the Doppler effect to a certain extent. For this modulation method, a two-level equalization scheme is proposed, which can guarantee the performance of the above two advantages to a certain extent, and the D-F, OFDM and OTFS systems are compared under different channels from theory and simulation. The simulation results show that the D-F domain transmission achieves good performance in some channels.

Title: Image transmission in underwater acoustic communication channel using LDPC codes
Authors: Avik Kumar Das, Saikat Chandra Bakshi, Ankita Pramanik, P. Venkateswaran
Abstract: Traditional methods in underwater acoustic communication (UWAC) often struggle to overcome signal degradation and noise interference. This paper explores the application of low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes for improved image transmission in UWAC. The proposed work demonstrates the efficacy of a rate six by seven and a rate five by six LDPC codes in the UWAC channel to compensate the variable nature of noises in UWAC.

Title: Construction of New Finite Geometry Non-Binary LDPC Codes With Reduced Alphabets
Authors: Zakaria M'Rabet, Fouad Ayoub, Mostafa Belkasmi, Faissal El Bouanani
Abstract: The finite Euclidean and Projective geometry codes are the most known among One-Step Majority-Logic Decodable codes that are Low-Density Parity-Check codes as well. Unfortunately, these codes are rare, and we do not have a great diversity when designing communication systems with a fixed code-length and code rate. Regarding the non-binary Euclidian and projective geometric Codes, each code is restricted to a unique alphabet. To offer a bigger range of possible alphabets for each code, we propose new construction by redefining the incidence vector of the lines of Euclidean and projective geometries over finite fields. This approach leads to an enrichment of the class of EG and PG codes. An error performance study of some new codes has been carried out and shows good results.

Title: A New Hybrid Structure for Concatenated Codes
Authors: Ruonan Cheng, Lijun Zhang
Abstract: Erasure codes play an important role in improving network performance. Adding erasure codes to QUIC can effectively reduce data retransmission times, leading to better network performance. However, the complexity of the RS codes used in QUIC-FEC is high. Using short polar codes can reduce complexity. However, short polar codes have poor erasure correction performance. To solve this problem, we first proposed a method of combining (7, 4) Hamming codes with short polar codes in parallel. After providing protection for the incompletely polarized channels, the performance was significantly improved while maintaining low complexity and delay. Subsequently, based on Tanner diagrams of polar codes and Monte Carlo simulations, we determined the key bits that had the significant impact on the performance of the parallel concatenated codes. Therefore, we proposed a new parallel-serial hybrid structure, using (7, 4) Hamming codes to provide additional protection for these key bits. The results showed that the performance of the hybrid concatenated codes was better than that of the parallel concatenated codes, even better than the polar codes with the same bit rate and nearly twice the length of hybrid concatenated codes. The hybrid scenario also outperformed the RS codes used in QUIC protocol to restore lost packets. This new hybrid concatenated scheme can also be directly extended to a wider range of component codes.
